questions.question: Define Cartesian product of two sets A and B. If A = {1,2} and B = {a,b,c}, find A × B and B × A.
keyPoints: Definition: A × B = {(a,b) : a ∈ A and b ∈ B}
Note: A × B ≠ B × A unless A = B (order matters)

questions.question: Let R be a relation on set A = {1,2,3,4} defined by R = {(1,1), (1,2), (2,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4)}. Determine if R is: (i) Reflexive (ii) Symmetric (iii) Transitive
keyPoints: (i) Reflexive: Yes - contains (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4) for all elements
(ii) Symmetric: Yes - if (1,2) ∈ R then (2,1) ∈ R; similarly for other pairs
(iii) Transitive: Yes - whenever (a,b) and (b,c) ∈ R, (a,c) ∈ R. Examples: (1,1) and (1,2) gives (1,2); (1,2) and (2,1) gives (1,1)

questions.question: Explain the difference between domain and range of a relation with an example.
keyPoints: Domain: Set of all first components of ordered pairs in R
Range: Set of all second components of ordered pairs in R
Domain(R) = {1,2}, Range(R) = {x,y}
Domain is inputs, Range is outputs

questions.question: Define inverse relation R⁻¹. If R = {(1,2), (2,3), (3,4)}, find R⁻¹ and verify that (R⁻¹)⁻¹ = R.
keyPoints: Definition: R⁻¹ = {(b,a) : (a,b) ∈ R}
Thus inverse of inverse returns original relation

questions.question: What is an equivalence relation? Show that the relation 'congruence modulo n' on integers is an equivalence relation.
keyPoints: Equivalence relation: Relation that is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive
For n > 0, define a ≡ b (mod n) if n divides (a-b)
Reflexive: a ≡ a since n divides (a-a) = 0
Symmetric: If a ≡ b, then n|(a-b), so n|(b-a), hence b ≡ a
Transitive: If a ≡ b and b ≡ c, then n|(a-b) and n|(b-c), so n|(a-c), hence a ≡ c

questions.question: Define composition of relations R and S. If R = {(1,2), (2,3)} and S = {(2,1), (3,1)}, find R ∘ S.
keyPoints: Definition: R ∘ S = {(a,c) : ∃b such that (a,b) ∈ S and (b,c) ∈ R}
Note: Apply S first, then R
Check: For (a,b) ∈ S and (b,c) ∈ R
(3,1) ∈ S but no (1,c) ∈ R for any c, so not included
Result: R ∘ S = {(1,3)}

questions.question: Define partial order relation. Check whether relation '≤' on set of integers is a partial order.
keyPoints: Partial order: Relation that is reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive
Check for ≤ on integers:
Reflexive: For any integer a, a ≤ a (true)
Antisymmetric: If a ≤ b and b ≤ a, then a = b (true)
Transitive: If a ≤ b and b ≤ c, then a ≤ c (true)
Therefore, ≤ is a partial order relation. (ℤ, ≤) is a poset.

questions.question: Let R be a relation on A = {1,2,3,4} defined by R = {(1,1), (1,3), (2,2), (2,4), (3,1), (3,3), (4,2), (4,4)}. (i) Find domain and range of R. (ii) Determine if R is an equivalence relation. (iii) If yes, find equivalence classes.
keyPoints: (i) Domain = {1,2,3,4}, Range = {1,2,3,4} (all elements appear)
(ii) Check: Reflexive - contains (1,1), (2,2), (3,3), (4,4) ✓
Symmetric - (1,3)∈R implies (3,1)∈R ✓; (2,4)∈R implies (4,2)∈R ✓
Transitive - check: (1,1) and (1,3) gives (1,3) ✓; (1,3) and (3,1) gives (1,1) ✓; etc
Yes, R is an equivalence relation
(iii) Equivalence classes: [1] = {1,3}, [2] = {2,4}
These partition the set A into two equivalence classes

questions.question: (a) Define: (i) Symmetric relation (ii) Antisymmetric relation (iii) Transitive relation with one example each on set A = {1,2,3}. (b) Give an example of a relation that is both symmetric and antisymmetric.
keyPoints: (a)(i) Symmetric: (a,b)∈R ⇒ (b,a)∈R. Example: R = {(1,2),(2,1)}
(a)(ii) Antisymmetric: (a,b)∈R and (b,a)∈R ⇒ a=b. Example: R = {(1,2),(2,3)} is antisymmetric (no pair with reverse)
(a)(iii) Transitive: (a,b)∈R and (b,c)∈R ⇒ (a,c)∈R. Example: R = {(1,2),(2,3),(1,3)}
(b) Only relation that is both symmetric and antisymmetric is the identity relation I = {(a,a): a∈A} or empty relation. Because if (a,b) with a≠b exists, it creates contradiction.

questions.question: Prove that the relation '||' (parallel) on the set of lines in a plane is an equivalence relation. State which lines form the equivalence class of a given line l.
keyPoints: Define: Line l1 || l2 if they are parallel (including coincident lines)
Reflexive: Any line l is parallel to itself (l || l). ✓
Symmetric: If l1 || l2, then l2 || l1 (parallelity is mutual). ✓
Transitive: If l1 || l2 and l2 || l3, then l1 || l3 (all parallel to same direction). ✓
Therefore, '||' is an equivalence relation
Equivalence class of line l: All lines parallel to l (including l itself)
These are all lines with the same direction/slope in the plane

questions.question: Let R and S be relations from A to B. Prove or disprove: (i) (R ∪ S)⁻¹ = R⁻¹ ∪ S⁻¹ (ii) (R ∩ S)⁻¹ = R⁻¹ ∩ S⁻¹ (iii) If R ⊆ S then R⁻¹ ⊆ S⁻¹
keyPoints: (i) (R ∪ S)⁻¹ = R⁻¹ ∪ S⁻¹: PROVE
Let (b,a) ∈ (R∪S)⁻¹ ⇔ (a,b) ∈ R∪S ⇔ (a,b)∈R or (a,b)∈S
⇔ (b,a)∈R⁻¹ or (b,a)∈S⁻¹ ⇔ (b,a)∈R⁻¹∪S⁻¹
Hence equal
(ii) (R ∩ S)⁻¹ = R⁻¹ ∩ S⁻¹: PROVE (similar argument)
(iii) If R ⊆ S then R⁻¹ ⊆ S⁻¹: PROVE
If (b,a)∈R⁻¹ then (a,b)∈R⊆S, so (a,b)∈S, thus (b,a)∈S⁻¹
